original_abstract: This paper presents a critical discourse analysis (CDA) of the political speeches delivered by Barack Obama and Hassan Rouhani at the United Nations in September 2013. Drawing upon Halliday’s Systemic Functional Linguistics (SFL) as a theoretical framework, the study utilizes transitivity systems, modality, and personal pronouns to uncover how linguistic choices encode ideology and power. Results indicate that both leaders rely heavily on material processes to emphasize governmental action and leadership, though Obama’s discourse is characterized by more colloquial, accessible language compared to Rouhani’s formal and academic tone. Modality analysis reveals a high frequency of median-level politeness, specifically the use of "will" and "can," to project authority and future commitment. Furthermore, the strategic use of the first-person plural pronoun "we" is shown to serve the dual purpose of fostering intimacy with the audience while asserting institutional power. By examining these linguistic strategies, the study illustrates how political figures manufacture consensus and manifest their policy objectives through the "art of linguistic spin," ultimately making complex ideological positions more accessible to the general public.
humanized_abstract: This paper represents a critical discourse analysis (CDA) of the speeches delivered by the President of the United States, Barack Obama and the President of Iran, Hassan Rouhani in the United Nations on 24 and 24 September 2013 respectively. Using Halliday's Systemic Functional Linguistics (SFL) as theoretical perspective, the transitivity systems, modality and personal pronouns used in their speeches have been examined with the aim of elucidating how the leadership ideology and power are instantiated through language. The findings show that both leaders tend to use material processes that highlight governmental action and leadership. While Obama in his speech utilized more informal and popular language, his Speech was far more formal and scientific than Obama's. Also median level of politeness in terms of using "will" and "can" was common in their speeches. In addition the presidents used the first person plural pronoun "we" in order to develop solidarity with the audience while instantiating the institution power. Thus, by exploring the language structure used in presidential speeches, this research study aims at addressing the ways through which presidential speeches help to reach a consensus concerning the policy and on the achievement of aims through the so-called 'art of linguistic spin' in an attempt to convey abstract political ideas to ordinary people.
